g.data-narration: MIMD Systems form the backbone of modern parallel computing, enabling multiple processors to work independently.
MIMD stands for Multiple Instruction Multiple Data, where processors execute different instructions on different data simultaneously.
g.text: MIMD (Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data) is a parallel architecture where
multiple processors execute DIFFERENT instructions on DIFFERENT data
simaneously. Each processor has its own control unit and works independently.

g.data-narration: MIMD systems have three key characteristics: independent execution, asynchronous operation, and flexible programming capabilities.
SIMD executes the same instruction on multiple data elements in lockstep, while MIMD executes different instructions on different data asynchronously.
g.text: SIMD vs MIMD: The Fundamental Difference

g.data-narration: Shared memory MIMD systems have a global address space where all processors communicate through memory reads and writes.
Distributed memory MIMD systems have private local memory per processor and communicate through message passing over an interconnection network.
Shared memory offers simple programming with implicit communication but limited scalability. Distributed memory provides excellent scalability but requires explicit message passing.
g.text: Comparison: Shared vs Distributed Memory MIMD

g.data-narration: Interconnection networks connect processors and memory. Common topologies include bus, mesh, hypercube, and crossbar, each with different performance characteristics.
Cache coherence ensures data consistency across processor caches. Protocols include Write-Invalidate, Write-Update, MSI, and MESI.
MSI has three states: Modified (exclusive, dirty), Shared (clean, multiple copies), and Invalid (stale). MESI adds an Exclusive state for optimization.
OpenMP is a shared memory programming model using compiler directives. The pragma omp parallel creates concurrent threads.
MPI is a distributed memory programming model using explicit message passing. Processes communicate via send and receive operations.
Real-world MIMD systems include multicore processors (shared memory) and supercomputers like IBM Summit (distributed memory).
Key performance factors include load balancing, communication overhead, synchronization bottlenecks, and system scalability.
MIMD is the most versatile parallel architecture with independent processors executing different instructions on different data.
g.text: Summary: MIMD = Multiple Instruction, Multiple Data | Most Common & Flexible Parallel Architecture
